preFix.title: Fixing squeaky floorboards from above
preFix.subtitle: Stop floorboard squeak by screwing into joists from the top surface
preFix.excerpt: How to locate and eliminate squeaky floorboards without lifting them, using screws driven from the top surface into the joist below, covering squeak diagnosis, joist location, correct screw depth, and filling the screw heads.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (floor boarding). Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Wood Occupations.

content.text: A
floorboard squeak
is caused by a board that has worked loose from its
joist

. The original nails have worked loose over years of foot traffic and seasonal movement, allowing the board to flex. Driving a screw from the top surface into the joist clamps the board back down and eliminates the movement. The job takes around 15 minutes per board and requires no specialist tools beyond a cordless drill.
What you will need: materials

No. 8 x 50 mm countersunk wood screws (the standard floor-fixing screw). One-coat filler for filling the screw heads. For a carpeted floor, the screws can be left slightly recessed and the carpet re-laid over them; for a painted or varnished floor, the screw heads need filling and sanding flush. A cable-and-pipe detector.
Step 1, check for hidden services before drilling

Before drilling any screw hole, sweep a cable-and-pipe detector along the line of the joist in both directions. Cables and pipes often run at right angles to the joists through notches cut in the joist top edge, and occasionally run alongside the joist in the cavity below. A detector hit within 50 mm of the planned screw position is enough reason to move the fixing 100 mm to one side. Do not guess: the penalty for drilling through a cable is significant.
Step 2, locate the joist

Joists run at 90 degrees to the floor boards. In most UK homes built before 1980, they are spaced at 400 mm centres; in newer homes at 600 mm. Look for the row of nails along the board surface: the nails are driven into the joist and their line of dots marks the joist centre. If the original nails are not visible (they may be filled and painted), tap across the board with a knuckle: a hollow sound is the space between joists, a dull thump is directly over the joist. Confirm with a thin probe-hole before drilling the full fixing screw.
Step 3, pre-drill the countersink hole

Pre-drill a pilot hole and countersink at the screw position. The pilot hole should be slightly smaller than the screw shank and the countersink deep enough to seat the screw head 2 mm below the board surface. Avoid driving the screw directly without pre-drilling: the board may split, particularly in older dry timber, and a split runs along the grain in an unpredictable direction.
Step 4, drive the screw

Drive the No. 8 x 50 mm screw through the board and into the joist. The screw needs to penetrate at least 25 mm into the joist to get a reliable grip. Tighten until the board surface pulls down firm against the joist; overtightening can pull through the countersink and split the board face. Drive two or three screws along the squeaky section at 150 to 200 mm spacing if the board is loose over a longer run.
Step 5, test and fill the screw heads

Walk the repaired section: the squeak should be gone. If it persists, the screw missed the joist or the joist itself is moving. Add a second screw 50 mm away; if the problem persists, the joist end may have lifted off its bearing and requires a structural inspection. For boards that will be visible (stripped, stained, or painted), fill the screw heads with one-coat filler, allow to dry, sand flush with the sanding block, and refinish to match.
Variations

For a thin parquet or hardwood floor where a 50 mm screw is too long, use a 38 mm No. 8 screw and angle it slightly to catch the joist. For a floor where the squeak is between two boards rather than between a board and a joist (the board edges are rubbing), inject a small amount of wood glue or powdered graphite into the joint between the boards; the graphite lubricates and eliminates the friction squeak without fixing the boards together permanently.
Care

Screwed floorboards do not re-squeak under normal use. The screws outlast the original nails by decades. If a new squeak appears at a different point, repeat the diagnosis and fixing process. A floor that develops widespread squeaking is a sign of structural movement or moisture change in the floor void and warrants investigation before fixing individual boards.

attrs.fix: Probe the joist position more carefully before adding a second screw. Try tapping gently with a knuckle to find the solid centre. Add the second screw 30 mm to one side.
attrs.cause: Screw missed the joist centre; only hitting the edge of the joist gives limited clamping force.

attrs.fix: Withdraw the screw immediately. Drill a larger pilot hole. Apply a drop of wood glue to the split and clamp or tape the split board face closed while it cures. Re-drive with a screw slightly offset from the split.
attrs.cause: Pilot hole too small, or no pilot hole; screw driving too fast in old dry timber.
attrs.symptom: Board splits as the screw is driven

attrs.fix: Remove the screw, fill the over-drilled countersink with filler, allow to cure, and re-drill to a shallower depth. In very soft joists, a larger-diameter washer-head screw gives better pull-through resistance.
attrs.cause: Countersink too deep; old soft joist timber.
attrs.symptom: Screw head pulls below the surface of the board and into the joist
